role_summary: Civil rights physician; founder of the Tufts-Delta Health Center at Mound Bayou, Mississippi (1965) and the federal community-health-center model; co-founder of Physicians for Human Rights; founding chair of CUNY Medical School's Community Health and Social Medicine department.

photo.alt: H. Jack Geiger, May 2012 (screen capture from UC Irvine lecture)
photo.photographer: University of California, Irvine (Quest of Peace initiative)

ai_reading: Geiger's strongest semantic neighbor in this corpus is Alfred Moldovan (entry 133), where the embedding counts 443 near-passages, far above his next neighbor and the highest single pairing in his row. The size of that link is striking because Moldovan sits at the catalog's ingestion-only tier and was a New York transfusion specialist rather than a community-health-center builder; the two never co-founded an institution, yet cosine similarity binds their testimony tightly. The shared signal is the Medical Committee for Human Rights vocabulary that runs through both interviews, and Moldovan's own interview names Geiger directly (entry 133, 00:51:20), which is the kind of mutual reference the embedding rewards. Below Moldovan the row falls off to William G. Anderson (entry 126, count 233), Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 188), and Robert L. Carter (entry 108, count 178), with Maria Varela (entry 81) and Norma Mtume (entry 93) tied at 171; the cluster mixes movement physicians with Mississippi voter-registration organizers rather than grouping Geiger with other doctors alone. On the five concept axes his raw positions are all within about three hundredths of zero (nonviolence-self-defense -0.007, sacred-secular -0.020, tactical-strategic -0.030, southern-northern -0.033, individual-collective +0.018), which places his testimony among the most centrist readings in the audited subset; the embedding does not pull his account toward any single pole, consistent with an interview that moves across South Africa, Boston, the Mississippi Delta, and federal health policy in turn.
biographical_paragraph: Geiger took an MD at Case Western Reserve University School of Medicine in 1958 and a master's in epidemiology at the Harvard School of Public Health [src: 2]. In 1965 he founded the Tufts-Delta Health Center at Mound Bayou, Mississippi, one of the first federally funded community health centers; the model he developed for low-income community medical care became the basis for what is now a national network of community health centers [src: 2]. He later helped found Physicians for Human Rights and was the founding chair of CUNY Medical School's Community Health and Social Medicine department [src: 2]. His often-quoted line that the specific therapy for malnutrition is food framed the Mound Bayou practice of writing food orders that the health center paid for out of its pharmacy budget, a practice he set down in his own 1969 Health/PAC account of the center's first years [src: 7]. That formulation does not appear in his Library of Congress oral history; in the 2013 interview he describes the same food-as-medicine logic through the center's farm cooperative and emergency food program rather than the prescription-pad anecdote [src: 1]. He was born November 11, 1925 in Brooklyn, New York, and died December 28, 2020 in Brooklyn at ninety-five; the New York Times obituary by Denise Grady is the standard published record of his life [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 54 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039); the interview was conducted by historian John Dittmer in 2013 [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: H. Jack Geiger
interview_snippets.lead_in: Describing the Pholela health centre in South Africa, where staff handed out skim milk to a community riddled with malnutrition, Geiger draws the line forward to his later work in the Mississippi Delta:
interview_snippets.quote: They were distributing skim milk for which the South African government accused them of communism and this will come up again in Mississippi.

interview_snippets.speaker: H. Jack Geiger
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the recruitment of Northern white students for the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer, and the calculation he saw behind it:
interview_snippets.quote: this was going to be effective because white lives were valuable. And all of these white kids were coming down from the north and the country up until then hadn't cared very much if black kids were killed or black adults for that matter or black anything.

interview_snippets.speaker: H. Jack Geiger
interview_snippets.lead_in: Explaining why he steered the Office of Economic Opportunity grant toward the Mississippi Delta rather than a less hostile site:
interview_snippets.quote: And what everybody perceived as the belly of the beast, the worst of the problems and the worst of the racism and the worst of the need.

interview_snippets.speaker: H. Jack Geiger
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the legal opening that let a Massachusetts university fund a health center in Mississippi over the objections of the governor and Senator John C. Stennis:
interview_snippets.quote: We were accused with some correctness of having reinvented carpet bagging. But what happened was the governor Johnson, Senator Stennis and everybody else suddenly discovered that we were veto proof.

interview_snippets.speaker: H. Jack Geiger
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the North Bolivar County farm cooperative the health center organized, treating food production as part of medical care:
interview_snippets.quote: started to sell surplus in the open local market and even had dreams for a while of getting a cannery and growing soul food it could be canned and sold in the northern ghettos

interview_snippets.speaker: Alfred Moldovan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Alfred Moldovan, a fellow Medical Committee for Human Rights physician whose testimony the embedding places closest to Geiger's in this corpus, recalls Geiger among the doctors who built lasting health institutions in the South:
interview_snippets.quote: I remember Jack Geiger, who was a very important writer and ideologue for medical care.

movement_context: Geiger's community-health-center practice traces to a 1957-1958 year he spent at the Pholela Community Health Centre in the Drakensberg foothills of Natal, South Africa, working with Sidney L. Kark and Emily Kark on the social-medicine model the Karks had been developing since the centre's 1940 founding under the South African Department of Health; the Pholela model combined household-level epidemiology, community-defined health priorities, and clinical care of the residents into a single integrated programme that became the template Geiger carried back to the United States [src: 3, 4]. Geiger was a Tufts University medical-school faculty member when he and Count D. Gibson Jr., then chair of Tufts Department of Preventive Medicine, secured the first two demonstration-project grants of the federal Office of Economic Opportunity's neighborhood-health-center program in 1965, one for a Columbia Point housing project in Boston, the other for Mound Bayou and the surrounding northern Bolivar County region of the Mississippi Delta [src: 5, 6]. Sargent Shriver, the OEO director, had funded the neighborhood-health-center demonstration line out of the War on Poverty appropriation under Title II of the Economic Opportunity Act of 1964; the program scaled from the two Tufts demonstration grants in 1965 to roughly one hundred federally funded centers by 1971 and is the institutional ancestor of the present-day federally qualified community health center (FQHC) network now serving approximately thirty million Americans annually [src: 5, 6]. The Mound Bayou centre opened in November 1967 in a converted former movie theatre; its prescription-pad-for-food practice, written into the pharmacy budget on the legal theory that food is the specific therapy for malnutrition, drew an OEO audit and a 1969 federal review that ultimately validated the practice [src: 6, 7]. The June 1964 founding of the Medical Committee for Human Rights (MCHR) in Dr. Tom Levin's New York apartment, which Geiger participated in alongside roughly one hundred Northern physicians, nurses, psychologists, and social workers, was the organizational precursor that connected the Northern medical-faculty cohort to the Mississippi Freedom Summer project and set the cohort relationships that would carry into the 1965 Mound Bayou founding [src: 8, 9]. Geiger went on to co-found Physicians for Social Responsibility (PSR) in 1961 with Bernard Lown, Victor Sidel, and others; PSR's parent body, the International Physicians for the Prevention of Nuclear War (IPPNW), received the 1985 Nobel Peace Prize, with Geiger present at the Oslo December 10, 1985 ceremony as part of the PSR delegation [src: 10]. In 1986 Geiger co-founded Physicians for Human Rights (PHR) with Jonathan Fine, Jane Schaller, Carola Eisenberg, Jennifer Leaning, and Robert Lawrence; PHR shared the 1997 Nobel Peace Prize with the International Campaign to Ban Landmines for its work documenting the medical-and-public-health consequences of antipersonnel mines [src: 11].
legacy_and_reception: Geiger left the Tufts faculty in 1971 for SUNY Stony Brook and in 1978 joined the new CUNY Medical School at the City College of New York (the Sophie Davis School of Biomedical Education), where he served as founding chair of the Department of Community Health and Social Medicine until his 2001 retirement and continued teaching as the Arthur C. Logan Professor Emeritus of Community Medicine [src: 2, 11]. The Delta Health Center, the operational continuation of the 1965 Tufts-Delta demonstration project, has remained continuously open at Mound Bayou and now operates fifteen clinical sites across northern Mississippi as a federally qualified community health center; the centre celebrated its fiftieth anniversary in 2017 with a documentary, Out in the Rural, and a fiftieth-anniversary scholarly volume [src: 6, 12]. The Geiger Gibson Program in Community Health Policy was founded at George Washington University's Milken Institute School of Public Health in 2003 (with planning starting in 2001) to honor Geiger and Gibson and to train health-policy researchers in the community-health-center model the two physicians had originated; the program produces the annual Geiger Gibson / RCHN Community Health Foundation Research Collaborative reports on federally qualified community health centers [src: 13]. Geiger died December 28, 2020 in Brooklyn at ninety-five; the 2009 John Dittmer monograph The Good Doctors: The Medical Committee for Human Rights and the Struggle for Social Justice in Health Care, published by Bloomsbury Press (paperback reprint University Press of Mississippi, 2017), remains the standard institutional history of MCHR and is the principal published source on Geiger's role in the 1964 Tom Levin-apartment founding, the 1965 Mound Bayou demonstration grant, and the larger MCHR-to-OEO arc that produced the federal community-health-center program [src: 9].

gallery.alt: Isaiah Thornton Montgomery House, West Main Street, Mound Bayou, Bolivar County, Mississippi (Historic American Buildings Survey photograph)
gallery.caption: Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS) photograph of the Isaiah Thornton Montgomery House on West Main Street in Mound Bayou, Bolivar County, Mississippi (built c. 1910; HABS reference MISS,6-MOBA,1-2; National Register of Historic Places reference 76001092). Mound Bayou, the all-Black town founded in 1887 by formerly enslaved cousins Isaiah Thornton Montgomery and Benjamin T. Green on land purchased from the Louisville, New Orleans and Texas Railway, was the location Geiger and Count Gibson selected in 1965 for the second of two Office of Economic Opportunity neighborhood-health-center demonstration grants; the Tufts-Delta Health Center opened in November 1967 in a former Mound Bayou movie theatre.
gallery.photographer: Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS), National Park Service
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division, HABS collection)
gallery.license: Public domain (work of U.S. federal government employee in the course of official duties; 17 U.S.C. 105)

gallery.alt: R. Sargent Shriver, first Director of the Peace Corps, in a 1961 portrait by Rowland Scherman
gallery.caption: Rowland Scherman's 1961 portrait of R. Sargent Shriver, taken during Shriver's directorship of the Peace Corps. Shriver subsequently served as the first director of the federal Office of Economic Opportunity (OEO) from October 1964 through March 1968 under Presidents Lyndon Johnson and Richard Nixon; the OEO's neighborhood-health-center demonstration line, authorized under Title II of the Economic Opportunity Act of 1964 (P.L. 88-452), funded the two 1965 Tufts demonstration projects co-directed by H. Jack Geiger and Count Gibson, one at Columbia Point in Boston and the other at Mound Bayou in the Mississippi Delta. The program scaled to roughly one hundred federally funded centers by 1971 and is the institutional ancestor of the present-day federally qualified community health center network. Image from the John F. Kennedy Presidential Library and Museum, R. Sargent Shriver Collection.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via John F. Kennedy Presidential Library and Museum, R. Sargent Shriver Collection)
gallery.license: Public domain (PD-USGov; work of U.S. federal government employee in the course of official duties; 17 U.S.C. 105)

gallery.alt: Physicians for Social Responsibility delegation at the December 10, 1985 Nobel Peace Prize ceremony in Oslo; H. Jack Geiger is among the thirteen identified attendees
gallery.caption: Physicians for Social Responsibility (PSR) delegation at the Nobel Peace Prize ceremony in Oslo on December 10, 1985. PSR's parent body, the International Physicians for the Prevention of Nuclear War (IPPNW), received the 1985 Nobel Peace Prize for its work on the medical consequences of nuclear war. Henry David Abraham, the photographer, holds the Nobel medal in this image; the diploma sits on the table. The thirteen identified attendees include Thomas Chalmers, Herbert Abrams, Victor Sidel, Jennifer Leaning, Sidney Alexander, and H. Jack Geiger. PSR was founded in 1961 by Bernard Lown, Victor Sidel, Geiger, and others; Geiger later co-founded a parallel organization, Physicians for Human Rights, in 1986, which shared the 1997 Nobel Peace Prize with the International Campaign to Ban Landmines.

sources.title: H. Jack Geiger oral history (interview by John Dittmer, 2013)
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039; collection authorized by the 2009 Civil Rights History Project Act)

sources.title: Denise Grady, Dr. H. Jack Geiger, Doctor Who Fought Social Ills, Dies at 95
sources.publisher: The New York Times, December 28, 2020 (the canonical published obituary of record documenting the November 11, 1925 Brooklyn birth, the December 28, 2020 Brooklyn death at ninety-five, the Case Western Reserve MD and Harvard School of Public Health epidemiology master's, the Tufts-Delta Health Center founding, the CUNY Medical School Sophie Davis chair, and the Physicians for Human Rights co-founding)

sources.title: Sidney L. Kark and Guy W. Steuart (eds.), A Practice of Social Medicine: A South African Team's Experiences in Different African Communities
sources.publisher: E. and S. Livingstone, Edinburgh, 1962; WorldCat OCLC 40193738 (the foundational published account of the Pholela Community Health Centre's 1940-1959 social-medicine programme co-authored by its founding director Sidney Kark; Geiger spent 1957-1958 at Pholela learning the Kark model that he later adapted at Mound Bayou)

sources.title: Mervyn Susser, A South African Odyssey in Community Health: A Memoir of the Discovery of Health Care at Pholela and Alexandra Health Centres, 1942-1959
sources.publisher: American Journal of Public Health (AJPH) monograph reprint, 1993; WorldCat OCLC 34921770 (the scholarly memoir of the Pholela and Alexandra Health Centre programmes by Mervyn Susser, the South African epidemiologist who worked with Sidney and Emily Kark at Pholela in the years Geiger visited)

sources.title: Sar A. Levitan, The Great Society's Poor Law: A New Approach to Poverty
sources.publisher: Johns Hopkins University Press, 1969; WorldCat OCLC 1029786 (the canonical institutional history of the federal Office of Economic Opportunity (OEO) 1964-1968, including the Title II neighborhood-health-center demonstration line that funded the two 1965 Tufts demonstration projects at Columbia Point and Mound Bayou under Sargent Shriver's directorship)

sources.title: Thomas J. Ward Jr., Out in the Rural: A Mississippi Health Center and Its War on Poverty
sources.publisher: Oxford University Press, 2017; WorldCat OCLC 1107341544 (the standard institutional history of the Tufts-Delta Health Center at Mound Bayou from its 1965 OEO demonstration-grant origin through its 1967 opening, the fifty-year operational record of the renamed Delta Health Center as a federally qualified community health center, and the prescription-pad-for-food practice that drew the 1969 federal audit)

sources.title: H. Jack Geiger, Hidden Hunger and Health: A Doctor in Rural Mississippi (Health/PAC Bulletin, March 1969)
sources.publisher: Health/PAC Bulletin, March 1969; WorldCat OCLC 2245430 (Geiger's own published account of the 1965-1969 Mound Bayou prescription-pad-for-food practice, the OEO budget rationale for treating food as the specific therapy for malnutrition under the pharmacy line, and the 1969 federal review of the practice; the primary-source published article most frequently cited in subsequent community-health-policy literature)

sources.title: Medical Committee for Human Rights (institutional entry)
sources.publisher: SNCC Digital Gateway, Duke University Libraries (the SNCC-organized digital-archive entry on MCHR, documenting the June 24, 1964 founding meeting at Dr. Tom Levin's New York home, the Mississippi Freedom Summer medical-support deployment, the four-point service program, the September 1964 national formalization, and the local-affiliate structure)

sources.title: John Dittmer, The Good Doctors: The Medical Committee for Human Rights and the Struggle for Social Justice in Health Care
sources.publisher: Bloomsbury Press, 2009 (paperback reprint University Press of Mississippi, 2017); WorldCat OCLC 427559834 (the standard institutional history of the Medical Committee for Human Rights from its June 1964 founding through its 1965 Selma deployment and its post-1968 Chicago Democratic Convention work; the principal published source on Geiger's role in the MCHR-to-OEO arc that produced the federal community-health-center program; written by the historian who later conducted Geiger's 2013 LoC oral history)

sources.title: The Nobel Peace Prize 1985: International Physicians for the Prevention of Nuclear War
sources.publisher: Nobel Foundation (the institutional record of the 1985 Nobel Peace Prize awarded to the International Physicians for the Prevention of Nuclear War (IPPNW), the parent body of Physicians for Social Responsibility (PSR) which Geiger co-founded in 1961 with Bernard Lown, Victor Sidel, and others)

sources.title: Physicians for Human Rights, Our Story (institutional history)
sources.publisher: Physicians for Human Rights (the institutional record of PHR's 1986 founding by Jonathan Fine, H. Jack Geiger, Jane Schaller, Carola Eisenberg, Jennifer Leaning, and Robert Lawrence, and of the organization's shared 1997 Nobel Peace Prize with the International Campaign to Ban Landmines)

sources.title: Delta Health Center, Our History
sources.publisher: Delta Health Center, Inc., Mound Bayou, Mississippi (institutional history of the 1965 founding by Tufts physicians H. Jack Geiger and Count Gibson with Office of Economic Opportunity funding; documents the Delta Health Center's standing as the first rural community health center and the first federally qualified community health center in the United States, the November 1967 opening at the converted Mound Bayou movie theatre, and the present-day fifteen-clinical-site northern-Mississippi operation)

sources.title: Geiger Gibson Program in Community Health Policy
sources.publisher: Milken Institute School of Public Health, George Washington University (institutional record of the program founded in 2003 to honor H. Jack Geiger and Count Gibson and to train health-policy researchers in the community-health-center model; produces the annual Geiger Gibson / RCHN Community Health Foundation Research Collaborative reports on federally qualified community health centers)
